scene_title: A lane by the wall of Capulet's orchard

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Can I go forward when my heart is here?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Turn back, dull earth, and find thy centre out. He climbs the wall, and leaps down within it.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Romeo! my cousin Romeo!

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: He is wise;

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: And, on my lie, hath stol’n him home to bed.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: He ran this way, and leap’d this orchard wall:

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Call, good Mercutio.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Nay, I’ll conjure too.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Romeo! humours! madman! passion! lover!

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Appear thou in the likeness of a sigh:

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Speak but one rhyme, and I am satisfied;

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Cry but “Ay me!” pronounce but “love” and “dove;”

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Speak to my gossip Venus one fair word,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: One nick-name for her purblind son and heir,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Young Adam Cupid, he that shot so trim,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: When King Cophetua loved the beggar-maid!

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: He heareth not, he stirreth not, he moveth not;

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: The ape is dead, and I must conjure him.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: I conjure thee by Rosaline’s bright eyes,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: By her high forehead and her scarlet lip,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: By her fine foot, straight leg and quivering thigh

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: And the demesnes that there adjacent lie,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: That in thy likeness thou appear to us!

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: And if he hear thee, thou wilt anger him.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: This cannot anger him: ’twould anger him

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: To raise a spirit in his mistress’ circle

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Of some strange nature, letting it there stand

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Till she had laid it and conjured it down;

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: That were some spite: my invocation

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Is fair and honest, and in his mistress’ name

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: I conjure only but to raise up him.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Come, he hath hid himself among these trees,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: To be consorted with the humorous night:

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Blind is his love and best befits the dark.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: If love be blind, love cannot hit the mark.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Now will he sit under a medlar tree,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: And wish his mistress were that kind of fruit

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: As maids call medlars, when they laugh alone.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: O, Romeo, that she were, O, that she were

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: An open-arse, thou a pop’rin pear!

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Romeo, good night: I’ll to my truckle-bed;

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: This field-bed is too cold for me to sleep:

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Come, shall we go?

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Go, then; for ’tis in vain

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: To seek him here that means not to be found.

glosses.definition: Romeo's own body, heavy with love
find your gravitational center — Juliet
summon by charm; Mercutio mocks Romeo's love-trance as possession
alas — stock lover's sigh
symbol of love; a lover's conventional exclamation
familiar Venus — the goddess of love, treated with casual disrespect
Cupid, Venus's half-blind boy
Cupid confused with Adam Bell, a legendary archer of ballad
legendary king who fell in love with a beggar-maid — popular ballad subject
the poor girl of the Cophetua ballad
moves; shows himself
the trickster's gone silent — performer's slang for an unresponsive audience
Mercutio doesn't know Romeo has moved on from Rosaline
typically Petrarchan blazon — cataloguing the beloved's features
bawdy — Mercutio pushes the Petrarchan blazon into obscenity
the regions next to — i.e., the genitals; explicit sexual joke

glosses.phrase: raise a spirit in his mistress’ circle
glosses.definition: double entendre — conjuring a spirit / sexual penetration
malice; vexation
damp, moody night — the humors of damp air
tree bearing a fruit whose eaten-ripe state was called 'open-arse' — bawdy joke
vulgar folk-name for the medlar fruit — Mercutio pushes the joke to limit
a variety of pear whose shape suggested the male anatomy
low wheeled bed that rolled under a higher one
camp-bed (here, the ground)

annotations.title: Mercutio's mock conjuration — the Petrarchan blazon weaponised
annotations.body: Mercutio's 'conjuration' of Romeo by cataloguing Rosaline's body is a deliberate parody of the Petrarchan blazon — the sonnet-convention of listing the beloved's features (bright eyes, red lips, white hand) as isolated ornaments. Mercutio pushes the form to obscenity ('quivering thigh / And the demesnes that there adjacent lie'), revealing what the blazon always half-concealed: the beloved's body reduced to a pornographic inventory. By degrading Petrarchism into filth here, Shakespeare clears the air for the balcony scene that follows — where Romeo and Juliet will speak to each other as subjects, not as catalogued objects. Mercutio's wit is the play's most relentless de-romanticising voice; its silence after 3.1 is a formal loss, not just the loss of a friend.
sources: Nancy J. Vickers, 'Diana Described: Scattered Woman and Scattered Rhyme' (1981)

trials.prompt: What does Mercutio's crude 'conjuration' of Rosaline parody?
options: The Petrarchan blazon — the sonnet-catalogue of a beloved's isolated features

trials.prompt: Why does Mercutio not understand Romeo's true condition in this scene?
options: He has not yet met Romeo
He still thinks Romeo pines for Rosaline — unaware of Juliet or the Capulet ball's outcome
